title: Combining two colonies using the newspaper method
subtitle: Uniting a weak colony with a strong one safely.
excerpt: A failing colony, queenless, low on bees, or struggling through winter, can be united with a stronger one to prevent it dying out entirely. The newspaper method slows the union down so the two sets of bees adjust to each other gradually, rather than fighting.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), colony-management section. BBKA technical sheet on uniting colonies. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A full social unit of bees: a laying queen, worker bees, drones, and developing brood all occupying one hive.
A colony with no laying queen; a colony in this state will produce no new workers and will dwindle within weeks.

content.text: is most often done in autumn, when a

colony or a colony too small to overwinter on its own needs to be merged into a healthy one. The newspaper acts as a slow barrier, the bees chew through it over the course of a day or two, mixing their scents as they go. By the time they meet face-to-face, they smell like the same colony and accept each other.

Decide which queen to keep. Keep the younger, better-tempered, higher-performing queen. If one colony is queenless, the decision is already made. If both have queens, find and remove the one you're not keeping before combining, otherwise the bees will do it for you by balling one of them, and you may lose both.

Move the weaker colony's hive to sit directly beside the stronger one, or swap it one metre per day for several days until they're adjacent. This reduces the number of flying bees that return to the old site after the union.
Close the entrance of the weaker colony to a single bee-space for the evening before combining. This calms the bees.

Remove the roof and crown board from the stronger hive. Lay a single sheet of newspaper across the top of the brood box, held in place by a light puff of smoke around the edge. Pierce the paper several times with your hive tool, four or five small holes is enough to allow scent to pass through.
Carry the weaker colony's brood box and set it directly on top of the newspaper-covered stronger hive. Add its crown board and roof.
Leave undisturbed for at least three days.

After three days, open and check. You'll find scraps of chewed newspaper at the bottom of the hive, a good sign. The bees should be moving freely between the two boxes. Over the following week, consolidate the frames from both boxes into one brood box and remove the second. Keep the frames with the most brood and the most stores. Return empty or near-empty frames for cleaning by the bees before storage.

attrs.title: After the union: reducing down for winter

content.text: Once combined, the merged colony will be larger than each was individually, but don't let it winter on two brood boxes if you can help it. A double-brood colony is harder to inspect and harder to prepare for winter. Aim to have all the bees on a single well-stocked brood box with 15,20 kg of stores by late September.

attrs.cue: Bees fighting at the joint between the two boxes
attrs.cause: The newspaper was chewed through too quickly, or the holes were too large
attrs.fix: Use a thicker piece of newspaper next time, and make fewer holes. If fighting is severe, separate the boxes, smoke both entrances, and retry with two sheets of paper.

attrs.cue: The queen is not found after combining
attrs.cause: She may have been balled by the other colony's workers
attrs.fix: Look for eggs after a week. If there are no eggs and no queen cells, the colony is queenless; you'll need to introduce a new queen or a frame of young eggs from another hive.
